question: Kotler's three-level product model identifies which levels?
options: Basic, Standard, and Premium levels based on price tier
Core benefit (the fundamental need being met), Actual product (features, design, quality), and Augmented product (warranty, delivery, service, brand)
Physical, Digital, and Experiential product versions
Raw material, Work-in-progress, and Finished goods
explanation: Three product levels: (1) Core benefit — what the customer is really buying (a drill buyer wants holes, not a drill); (2) Actual product — the physical/digital product with features, design, quality, brand name, packaging; (3) Augmented product — additional services and benefits added to the actual product (warranty, installation, after-sale service, free returns). Competitors often match at the actual product level; differentiation frequently occurs at the augmented level.

question: Theodore Levitt's famous insight 'People don't want a quarter-inch drill; they want a quarter-inch hole' illustrates which product level concept?
options: The actual product level — emphasizing that the physical drill is most important

The core benefit level — customers buy the solution to their need (the hole), not the mechanism that delivers it; this framing expands the competitive set (any hole-making solution competes with drills) and shifts product development focus toward the outcome rather than the tool
The augmented product level — after-sale drill service is the most valuable element
Product line management — companies should expand from drills to all hole-making tools
explanation: Levitt's insight drives 'Jobs-to-Be-Done' thinking (Clayton Christensen): define your product by the job it does, not its physical form. A drill's core benefit is 'making holes' — so competitors include jigsaws, laser cutters, and hammers. More broadly: transportation companies compete with video conferencing (eliminate the need for travel); entertainment competes for time (Netflix, YouTube, Instagram, gaming are all competing for the same attention). Defining the core benefit correctly reveals true competitors and product innovation opportunities.

question: Amazon Prime's product offering includes: same-day delivery, video streaming, music streaming, photo backup, Prime Reading, Prime Gaming, and exclusive member discounts. Which product level does this bundle primarily represent?
options: Core benefit — Amazon's core benefit is fast delivery above all else

Augmented product — the streaming, gaming, reading, and extra services are all augmented features added beyond the actual product (retail e-commerce) to add value, increase switching costs, and justify the $139 annual fee; the actual product (e-commerce) could exist without any of these additions; they are the 'more than expected' tier that creates loyalty
Actual product — all Prime features are core to the shopping experience
None of these — digital services don't fit the three-level product model
explanation: Amazon Prime's strategy: load the augmented product tier with so many ancillary benefits that the $139 membership fee feels justified by services other than the core actual product. A Prime member who uses: Prime Video ($15/month value), Prime Music ($10/month value), fast delivery ($8/month value estimate) — perceives $33/month in value from a $11.58/month subscription. The augmented product creates the retention lock-in. This is why canceling Prime feels expensive even when a customer buys infrequently from Amazon.

question: A McDonald's meal has the following product levels: Core benefit = ?, Actual product = ?, Augmented product = ?. Which mapping is correct?
options: Core: fast service; Actual: food; Augmented: McCafé beverages

Core: quick, affordable sustenance when hungry (convenience + satiation); Actual: burger, fries, drink with consistent taste/temperature; Augmented: PlayPlace for kids, WiFi, mobile ordering, dollar menu value, drive-thru availability
Core: burgers; Actual: fast food industry membership; Augmented: marketing
Core: food; Actual: restaurant experience; Augmented: food
explanation: McDonald's product levels: Core = rapid satisfaction of hunger with affordable, predictable food (the job-to-be-done: 'I'm hungry, busy, and want something reliable and cheap quickly'). Actual = standardized menu items with consistent taste globally. Augmented = PlayPlace (families with kids), WiFi (extended dwell time), mobile app ordering, loyalty rewards program, Dollar Menu (value positioning), 24-hour locations. Competitors like Chick-fil-A compete at the augmented level (superior service courtesy) rather than challenging McDonald's core price/convenience position.

question: Zappos charges the same shoe prices as its competitors but generates exceptional customer loyalty and repeat purchase rates. The augmented product explains this. What elements of Zappos' augmented product create this loyalty?
options: Zappos has exclusive shoe brands not available at competitors

Zappos' competitive advantage is entirely in the augmented product layer: 365-day return policy (removes purchase risk entirely), free 2-way shipping, 24/7 phone customer service with no time limits, WOW customer service stories (e.g., sending flowers to bereaved customers, overnight delivery for wedding emergencies), and surprise upgrades to overnight shipping; the actual product (shoes) and core benefit (footwear) are identical to competitors; Zappos competes only on the service wrapper around the same actual products
Zappos has lower wholesale costs than competitors, enabling service investment
Augmented product elements like return policies don't affect purchase loyalty
explanation: Zappos is the canonical augmented product case study. Same shoes at same prices — but the experience of buying from Zappos is radically different. The 365-day return policy eliminates purchase risk (try shoes for a year, return free if you don't love them). No-script customer service trained to create 'WOW' moments. These augmented features cost money but drove: 75% of purchases from repeat customers, word-of-mouth acquisition (low CAC), and Amazon's $1.2B acquisition in 2009. Zappos proved that in commodity categories, the augmented product is the differentiation battleground.

question: A software company offers its SaaS product with: 30-day free trial (no credit card), 24/7 live chat support, free onboarding session, 99.9% uptime SLA, and dedicated customer success manager for enterprise accounts. Map these to product levels and identify which create the most differentiation.
options: All features are equally important; product level mapping is irrelevant for software

Core benefit: solving the specific business problem (e.g., managing projects). Actual product: the software features, UX, and performance. Augmented: free trial (reduces purchase risk), 24/7 support (reduces operational risk), onboarding session (reduces adoption failure risk), uptime SLA (reliability guarantee), CSM for enterprise (highest-touch service). The augmented elements create the most differentiation because competitors often match features (actual product) but excel or fail in service delivery (augmented product) — enterprise sales often won and lost on CSM quality and onboarding experience
The 30-day free trial is an actual product feature since it is part of the base offering
Uptime SLA is a core benefit, not an augmented product element
explanation: SaaS three-level mapping: Core = the job done (project management, CRM, analytics). Actual = features, UI/UX, performance. Augmented = trial (risk reduction), support (ongoing assistance), onboarding (success enablement), SLA (reliability commitment), CSM (enterprise relationship). Research consistently shows B2B SaaS churn is most often prevented at the augmented level: poor onboarding and lack of CSM guidance causes customers to fail to adopt the product, then churn — even when the actual product is excellent. The augmented product is where the actual customer success or failure happens.

question: A luxury hotel charges $800/night. A budget motel charges $80/night. Both have core benefit = 'a place to sleep.' How do the actual and augmented product levels justify the 10x price difference?
options: The 10x price is only justified by better bed quality at the luxury hotel

Actual product differences: room size, bed quality, bathroom design, toiletries quality, furnishings, view, noise isolation, cleanliness standards. Augmented product differences: valet parking, concierge service, spa access, in-room dining, turn-down service, pool/fitness center, brand reputation (status signal for business travel), loyalty points value, check-in/out flexibility, personalized greeting for returning guests; the price premium is justified by both the enhanced actual product AND the comprehensive augmented service bundle that creates a total experience
Budget and luxury hotels have identical actual products; only brand perception differs
The price difference is entirely explained by location rent premium with no product quality difference
explanation: The hotel price ladder from $80 to $800 is primarily augmented product value: the core (sleep) and even the actual product (bed quality) differences are real but don't account for 10x. At Four Seasons, the augmented product includes: anticipatory service (knowing repeat guests' preferences), immediate response to any request, personalized touches (room stocked with your preferred beverages before arrival), and the intangible value of being in a place that signals success/status to colleagues and clients. This experience sells the story of being the type of person who stays at Four Seasons — an identity product.

question: Spotify's 'Discover Weekly' playlist (personalized algorithm-generated playlist every Monday) is a feature of the actual product. But it functions more like an augmented product element that drives retention. Explain why product level boundaries are blurring in digital products.
options: Digital products don't have product levels — the three-level model only applies to physical goods

In digital products, actual and augmented product elements are inseparable because they can be continuously updated and personalized: Discover Weekly is technically an actual product feature (algorithm in the app), but functionally it serves an augmented role — reducing churn by creating weekly ritual engagement, differentiating Spotify from Apple Music (which lacks this feature), and creating a 'WOW' moment that increases NPS; digital products blur the three-level hierarchy because personalization makes features function as unique services to each individual user
Product levels in digital services are identical to physical goods with no differences
Spotify's algorithm is a core benefit, not an actual or augmented product element
explanation: Digital products collapse the three-level model because: (1) The product is continuously updated (not a fixed actual product); (2) Personalization makes each user's experience unique (individualized augmented product at scale); (3) Features and services are bundled at no incremental cost (no clear augmented product premium). Discover Weekly functions like a personalized concierge service (augmented) built directly into the actual product. This is the advantage of software: you can deliver augmented-level experiences at actual-product scale and cost. The competitive implication: competitors must replicate both the algorithm AND the data to match it.

question: A pharmaceutical company's drug core benefit is 'treating a disease.' The actual product is the chemical compound and dosage form. The augmented product includes: patient support programs, nurse hotlines, copay assistance cards, and physician education programs. Why does pharma invest so heavily in augmented product when patients don't choose their own drugs?
options: Pharmaceutical augmented product investment is pure regulatory compliance, not strategic

Multi-stakeholder augmented product strategy: the prescribing decision involves multiple parties (physician, patient, payer/insurer); physician education programs target the prescriber decision; patient support programs improve adherence (patients who stay on therapy longer = more revenue); copay assistance cards remove patient affordability barriers that could lead a physician to switch to alternatives; nurse hotlines reduce adverse event dropout; payers see better outcomes data (which influences formulary access); each augmented element serves a different stakeholder in the purchase/adherence chain
Augmented product is only relevant for consumer products, not healthcare
Patient support programs are only provided for ethical reasons, not commercial ones
explanation: Pharma augmented product is stakeholder-specific: physician detailing and samples (prescriber decision), patient support programs and hotlines (adherence and persistence — staying on drug = revenue), copay cards (insurance barrier removal — payer access), outcomes research (payer formulary placement). Each element serves a specific role in a complex B2B2C purchase chain. Drugs with superior augmented product programs achieve higher market share vs. clinically equivalent competitors because adherence differences drive real-world revenue. This is B2B marketing applied to healthcare through multiple stakeholder segments simultaneously.

question: A company's core product is a project management tool. It discovers that its most loyal customers primarily use the tool's integrations (Slack, Google Drive, Jira) rather than its native features. These integrations were in the augmented product tier. What strategic implication follows?
options: Remove the integrations since customers should use native features instead

The integrations are actually functioning as the core benefit for this customer segment (they're buying workflow integration, not project management per se); this insight requires reclassifying what constitutes the core benefit, actual product, and augmented product for different customer segments; the company should invest in expanding integrations, build an integration marketplace, and potentially reposition from 'project management software' to 'the hub that connects your entire workflow stack' — the product strategy should follow where actual customer value is being created, not where the product was originally designed to deliver it
Integrations are always the least important product level element
The company should force customers to use native features by limiting integration access
explanation: Customer value migration: when customers consistently derive their core value from augmented product elements, those elements ARE the core benefit for that segment. Slack discovered that its most valuable users used it as a 'command center' for external service notifications (GitHub commits, Jira tickets, Salesforce alerts) not just team messaging. This insight drove Slack's 'App Directory' strategy — becoming a workflow integration hub. The lesson: regularly audit WHERE customers actually derive value, which may shift over time from where the product was originally designed to deliver it.

question: An airline sells Business Class at 4x the economy price. Core benefit = air travel between two cities (same for both classes). How does the augmented product justify a 4x premium when the core benefit is identical?
options: Business Class has a fundamentally different core benefit than Economy

The entire premium is built on actual + augmented product layers: Actual: lie-flat bed, wider seat, better food, direct aisle access; Augmented: lounge access (work/relax before flight), priority check-in/boarding (time saved), dedicated check-in desks (experience quality), premium miles earning (loyalty program value), priority baggage handling (time saved on arrival), reliability of upgrade policies (corporate relationship), 'arrive rested for meetings' outcome (the real job-to-be-done for business travelers); the 4x premium purchases all of these combined, but the primary driver is the actual product improvement (lie-flat bed for overnight flights enables work productivity)
Business class passengers receive guaranteed priority seating on all flights
The 4x premium is purely due to lower load factor in business class — fewer seats = higher price allocation
explanation: Business class economics: the core benefit (flying city A to city B) is identical. The 4x premium is entirely actual + augmented product value. For overnight transatlantic flights: the lie-flat bed enables productive arrival (arrive in London rested, go directly to meetings at 9am) — this is a genuine productivity value proposition for business travelers. For short domestic flights: the premium is harder to justify (no lie-flat bed, 1-hour flight difference from coach is negligible) — which is why Business Class on short routes is dying and only survives on long-haul routes where the augmented product delivers real functional value.

question: A competitor enters a market and matches your company's actual product features exactly (same functionality, similar design, lower price). What is the strategic response that leverages product levels?
options: Immediately lower prices to match or beat the competitor's pricing

Compete on augmented product dimensions the competitor cannot quickly copy: deepen service quality, extend warranties, add customer success programs, build community/ecosystem, leverage existing customer data to personalize experiences; it is difficult to match the augmented product of an established player because it requires: trained service staff, customer relationship history, established partner integrations, community investment, and brand trust built over time; the competitor can copy features in months but can't copy 5 years of customer relationships and service infrastructure
Exit the market since feature parity means the competitor will always win on price
Focus entirely on developing new features the competitor doesn't have yet
explanation: When actual product features are matched, competition shifts to augmented product where incumbents have durable advantages. AWS vs. Google Cloud and Azure: all three offer similar compute, storage, and services. AWS maintained dominance through augmented product: largest partner ecosystem, deepest documentation, largest developer community, most mature security certifications. These took 10+ years to build and can't be replicated with money alone. The principle: invest in augmented product elements that require time and trust to build — they create the most durable competitive moats when actual products are easy to copy.

question: Tesla's physical car is the actual product. Tesla's augmented product includes: over-the-air software updates, Supercharger network access, Autopilot improvements via software update, mobile service (mechanics come to you), and a direct-to-consumer sales model. How does Tesla's augmented product create structural competitive advantages that traditional automakers cannot easily replicate?
options: Tesla's advantage is only in battery technology — the augmented product is not differentiated

OTA updates transform the actual product post-purchase (a 2020 Model 3 in 2024 has capabilities unavailable in 2020), making Tesla the only car that improves over time; the Supercharger network is a physical infrastructure moat requiring years of capital investment; mobile service eliminates dealership dependency; direct sales eliminates dealer markup and controls customer experience; each augmented element requires: software infrastructure, charging infrastructure, service network redesign, and regulatory navigation that traditional dealers and OEM relationships prevent legacy automakers from quickly replicating
Traditional automakers can replicate all Tesla augmented product advantages within 1-2 years
OTA updates are a minor augmented product feature with limited strategic value
explanation: Tesla's augmented product moat is structural. OTA updates: requires a software-first architecture that legacy automakers (who use 100+ ECUs from different suppliers) cannot quickly replicate — GM tried and failed to deliver OTA updates to Bolt owners because the architecture doesn't support it. Supercharger network: 45,000+ fast-charging stations globally, built over 10 years. Mobile service: requires renegotiating dealer franchise laws in most U.S. states (Tesla fought these legal battles for years). Each element represents years of investment and regulatory navigation — time-based moats, not just money-based moats.

question: A medical device company sells an insulin pump (actual product) that requires consumable supplies (insulin cartridges, infusion sets). The company's business model generates 80% of revenue from consumables. What three-level product insight does this reveal?
options: The insulin pump is the core product; consumables are unrelated to the product levels

The actual product (insulin pump) is designed as a platform that locks customers into a consumable revenue stream — the augmented product (compatibility, proprietary cartridge design, insulin management software) creates switching costs that make the consumable revenue stream captive; the core benefit (blood glucose management) could theoretically be delivered by competing systems, but the augmented product layer (proprietary consumable formats, integrated CGM data, physician familiarity with the specific device) creates barriers to switching; the consumable model is the razor-blades architecture applied to medical devices
80% revenue from consumables is a sign of weak actual product design
Consumables revenue is always more profitable than device revenue for unrelated reasons
explanation: Medical device razor-blades model: Abbott FreeStyle, Dexcom CGM, insulin pump manufacturers — all use proprietary consumable formats to create ongoing revenue streams from the installed base. The augmented product (software integration with hospital EMR systems, physician training and certification, patient education programs) deepens switching costs beyond just consumable format — a hospital that has trained 50 nurses on one insulin pump system and integrated it into clinical workflows won't switch easily even if a cheaper device enters the market. Augmented product creates institutional switching costs.

question: When a company adds services to a physical product (e.g., Rolls-Royce's 'Power by the Hour' model — charging airlines per hour of engine use instead of selling engines), what fundamental shift in product level strategy is occurring?
options: The company is reducing its core benefit to focus on service

Servitization: the company shifts from selling the actual product (engine) to selling the core benefit as a service (thrust/power/availability) — the risk transfers from buyer (who previously bought and maintained the engine) to seller (who now must ensure the engine works to get paid); Rolls-Royce's model: the airline pays only when the engine works, aligning incentives for reliability engineering; Rolls-Royce must now maintain the augmented product as its core profit driver, not the one-time actual product sale
Power by the Hour is a simple leasing arrangement with no strategic implications
Servitization only makes sense for manufacturing companies, not service businesses
explanation: Servitization is one of the most significant business model innovations: Rolls-Royce transformed from a products company (selling engines for $20M each) to a services company (charging $50/engine-hour of flight). This model aligns incentives: Rolls-Royce only gets paid when the engine works → they designed more reliable engines → fewer in-flight shutdowns → better airline operations. It also creates predictable recurring revenue vs. lumpy capital goods sales. IBM, Xerox ('pay per page'), and Caterpillar have all implemented servitization — converting capital goods to outcome-based services. The three product levels merge: the actual product IS the service delivery mechanism.
